coredata.dc:description: Photocatalytic reduction of CO2 exhibits unsatisfactory photocatalytic performance owing to the inefficient separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, low CO2 capture efficiency and limited visible light absorption on most photo-catalysts. Herein, TiF bridged IL-CuCQDs-F/TiO2 inverse opal composite (IO-CFTi) was constructed for boosting CO2 visible-photo reduction via slow photo effect. In this work, ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA(Cu)) and imidazole ionic liquid 1-(2-Hydroxyethyl)-3-methylimidazolium tetrafluoroborate ([HOEtMIM][BF4]) were employed to confine grow of IL-CuCQDs-F within TiO2 inverse opal supporter via TiF bonds connection. Unique IL-CuCQDs-F efficiently expended light absorption towards visible region, and the confined growth of IL-CuCQDs-F within the TiO2 inverse opal cavity achieved the photoelectric conversion and efficient CO2 capture. Moreover, their TiF bonding interface of IO-CFTi assisted photogenerated electron transportation from TiO2 to CO2 for its reduction in this system. Consequently, IO-CFTi achieved a substantially increased CO production rate of 78.1 μmol·h−1·g−1 with 98 % selectivity. This improved performance in CO2 photoreduction positions the nanocomposite as a promising material for preservation of the environment and conversion of energy.

head.ce:title: TiF bridged IL-CuCQDs-F/TiO2 inverse opal composite for boosting CO2 visible-photo reduction via slow photon effect

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photocatalytic reduction of CO2 exhibits unsatisfactory photocatalytic performance owing to the inefficient separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, low CO2 capture efficiency and limited visible light absorption on most photo-catalysts. Herein, TiF bridged IL-CuCQDs-F/TiO2 inverse opal composite (IO-CFTi) was constructed for boosting CO2 visible-photo reduction via slow photo effect. In this work, ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA(Cu)) and imidazole ionic liquid 1-(2-Hydroxyethyl)-3-methylimidazolium tetrafluoroborate ([HOEtMIM][BF4]) were employed to confine grow of IL-CuCQDs-F within TiO2 inverse opal supporter via TiF bonds connection. Unique IL-CuCQDs-F efficiently expended light absorption towards visible region, and the confined growth of IL-CuCQDs-F within the TiO2 inverse opal cavity achieved the photoelectric conversion and efficient CO2 capture. Moreover, their TiF bonding interface of IO-CFTi assisted photogenerated electron transportation from TiO2 to CO2 for its reduction in this system. Consequently, IO-CFTi achieved a substantially increased CO production rate of 78.1 μmol·h−1·g−1 with 98 % selectivity. This improved performance in CO2 photoreduction positions the nanocomposite as a promising material for preservation of the environment and conversion of energy.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: In the past few years, the CO2 emissions of fossil fuel combustion have significantly increased the CO2 concentration in our atmosphere. Various strategies have been explored to mitigate the environmental problems caused by excessive CO2 emissions [1–3]. CO is a key raw material in chemical synthesis, metallurgy, and pharmaceutical industries, and can also be used as a fuel in certain types of fuel cells. The photocatalytic conversion of CO2 into hydrocarbons or other carbon-based chemicals is a practical solution as it can generate chemical fuels to meet energy demands and alleviate environmental problems. In the process of CO2 photoreduction, a major focus of current research is how to effectively utilize photocatalysts to capture and reduce CO2, thereby enhancing the conversion rate of photocatalytic CO2 [4].

Among photocatalysts for CO2 reduction, TiO2 is frequently used as a photocatalyst because of its high redox potential, excellent photostability, cheap cost, and non-toxic nature [5]. Furthermore, researchers have combined TiO2 with cocatalysts that possess high conductivity and visible light absorption to accelerate the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, thereby improving catalytic performance in CO2 photocatalytic reduction [6,7]. Xie et al. combined a high light-absorption sheet MoO3-x with TiO2 nanotubes, expanding the visible light absorption, and achieving a CO2 to CO photoreduction yield of 12.26 μmol·g−1·h−1 [8]. To further enhance the effective nanoscale interfacial contact and improve the photogenerated electron separation ability of the composite, Bi et al. successfully adsorbed carbon quantum dots (CQDs) on TiO2, resulting in a CO2 to CO conversation yield of 46.21 μmol·g−1·h−1 [9]. However, owing to the inefficient interface connection between TiO2 and the introduced nanoscale cocatalyst, the separation efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs could not be greatly increased, which resulted in a limited catalytic performance. Moreover, those catalysts are limited by the low utilization of photons due to the catalytic refraction and scattering of photons. Additionally, conventional powder catalysts exhibit low CO2 capture efficiency. Therefore, enhancing the interface interaction between TiO2 and the cocatalyst to improve the light utilization and CO2 capture efficiency of the catalyst is still the key challenge in achieving the efficient photoreduction of CO2.

In this work, the IL-CuCQDs-F was uniformly integrated onto TiO2 inverse opal through a robust TiF bond to develop the IO-CFTi composite catalyst, which achieved efficient photocatalytic conversion of CO2 to CO. The unique IL-CuCQDs-F achieved extended visible light absorption and enhanced CO2 capture through the CuN bonds and imidazole groups. The three-dimensional ordered macroporous (3DOM) structure of IO-CFTi further improved the CO2 capture efficiency. Simultaneously, the slow photon effect triggered by TiO2 inverse opal overlapped with the expanded visible light absorption range of IL-CuCQDs-F, resulting in a significant increase in visible light utilization. The strategy provides a reference for the study of CO2 reduction photocatalysts.
The general characterization techniques are supplied in Supporting information.

1-hydroxyethyl-3-methylimidazole tetrafluoroborate ([HOEtMIM][BF4], 99 %) was obtained from Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Tetrabutyl titanate (TBOT, 99.5 %) and disodium copper ethylenediamine tetraacetate (EDTA(Cu), 99 %) were obtained from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Absolute ethanol (EtOH, 99 %) was supplied by Sichuan Xilong Science Co., Ltd. Polystyrene microspheres (PS, 99.99 %) come from Tianjin Besler Chromatographic Technology Development Center. Dimethyl silicone oil (99 %) was produced in Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory.

Fabrication of PS template: A 15 wt% PS suspension was prepared by using PS microspheres of various sizes dispersed in ultrapure water. PS emulsion droplets were prepared in batches by a handmade microfluidic chip machine. Specifically, the PS suspension and dimethyl silicone oil were pumped into the inner and outer tubes of the microfluidic chip (0.4 mL·h−1 for PS suspension and 1.3 mL·h−1 for dimethyl silicone oil) to obtain monodispersed PS emulsified drops [10]. The obtained PS emulsified droplets were collected in dimethyl silicone oil and transferred to an oven at 40 °C for 72 h to allow for slow evaporation of the water. The internal PS microspheres were self-assembled by hydrophobic forces to form a colloidal template with opal structure [11]. The templates were then aged for 6 h at 90 °C and subsequently washed several times with EtOH to eliminate any remaining dimethyl silicone oil before being stored for use.

Fabrication of TiO2 powder: TiO2 was manufactured using a low-temperature method according to the literature [12]. Initially, 8 mmol of TBOT was added into 10 mL of EtOH, followed by dropwise addition to 100 mL of ultrapure water with vigorous stirring for 24 h. The resulting white precipitate was suction filtered, heated at 80 °C for 3 days, washed with ultrapure water multiple times, and finally TiO2 powder was obtained after dried at 70 °C for 12 h.

Fabrication of CuCQDs and IL-CuCQDs-F: First, 5 mmol of EDTA(Cu) was heated at 250 °C in N2 environment for 2 h [13,14]. After cooling, the solid was ground and ultrapure water was added for 30 min of ultrasonic treatment. Then the brown clear liquid was obtained by centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 20 min. The obtained clear solution was filtered multiple times with a microporous filter (0.22 μm) and treated with a dialysis belt of 3500 Da for 72 h to remove impurities. The purified solution was dried for 48 h at 80 °C and for another 12 h at 70 °C in vacuum oven, resulting in the production of CuCQDs. The synthesis of IL-CuCQDS-F was similar to that of CuCQDs, except that 5 mmol EDTA(Cu) was replaced with a mixture of 5 mmol EDTA(Cu) and 10 wt% ionic liquid [HOEtMIM][BF4]. The obtained sample was designated as IL-CuCQDs-F.

Fabrication of IO-Ti, IO-CTi, IO-CFTi: IO-CFTi was synthesized according to SI (Scheme 1). After adding 8 mmol of TBOT to 1 mL of EtOH in a sealed environment with 3 h of vigorous stirring, the mixture was utilized as a precursor to infiltrate the PS template. Subsequently, the mixture was calcinated at 500 °C to remove the PS template completely [15]. The material was rinsed with ultrapure water and EtOH, dried at 70 °C for 12 h and denoted as IO-Ti.

The prepared IO-Ti was dissolved in a 10 mL solution of 1 wt% CuCQDs and heated in a hydrothermal reactor at 180 °C for 48 h [16]. The obtained material was dried and designated as IO-CTi. The synthesis method for IO-CFTi was identical to that of IO-CTi, except that the CuCQDs solution was replaced with IL-CuCQDs-F solution, and the other processes remained the same [17].

Fabrication of CFTi powder: The prepared TiO2 powder was immersed in an IL-CuCQDs-F solution, then heated at 180 °C for 24 h. Subsequently, it was vacuum-dried for 12 h at 70 °C to produce the CFTi powder.

The photocatalytic CO2 reduction was typically performed in a 500 mL quartz reaction vessel, and prepared photocatalyst (100 mg) was added. Before the light irradiation, the air was completely exhausted with N2 (99.9999 %), then CO2 (99.9999 %) was introduced into the photoreactor and continuously aerated for 30 min. The vessel was sealed after 3 cycles at room temperature. A 300 W xenon (Xe) lamp was used as the light source to initiate the photocatalytic reaction. After each reaction, the gas products were analysed by gas chromatography (GC, Agilent 7890B, column: HP PLOT-U, carrier gas: N2, FID detector) and quantitative analysis was carried out by external standard method. Fig. S1A–B shows the standard curves of CH4 and CO gases. Isotopic 13CO2 labelling is commonly used to determine the source of products in photocatalytic CO2 reduction. The origin of carbon species in photocatalytic CO2 reduction products on IO-CFTi was analysed by isotopic tracer on a GC-mass spectrometer (GC–MS-QP2020, SHIMADZU, 32 °C, injection temperature: 170 °C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Temperature programmed desorption experiments of CO2 desorption by TGA analysis
ce:section.ce:para: First, the IO-Ti, IO-CTi and IO-CFTi were subjected to surface treatment to ensure surface activity. Next, the reactor was purged with N2 to remove any residual air, and CO2 was then introduced into the photoreactor and continuously aerated for 30 min. Subsequently, the materials were loaded into a thermogravimetric analyser with a N2 flow rate of 40 mL/min and an initial temperature of 25 °C. The temperature was gradually increased to 260 °C at a rate of 5 °C/min. During the heating process, the mass changes of the materials were recorded, and the temperature ranges corresponding to the mass loss were analysed to determine the CO2 desorption temperature. Through comparative analysis, the CO2 adsorption capacity and desorption characteristics of the materials can be evaluated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The characterization of IL-CuCQDs-F/TiO2 composite with inverse opal structure
ce:section.ce:para: The SEM images of the PS template, IO-Ti, IO-CTi, and IO-CFTi were displayed in Fig. 1 A, respectively. The PS template with a size of 250 nm demonstrated a regular opal structure [18]. After filling the PS template with precursor TBOT and then heat treatment, the IO-Ti (TiO2 inverse opal) with a highly ordered skeleton was obtained in Fig. 1B. Due to the shrinkage of the IO-Ti during calcination, the pore diameter of IO-Ti (∼240 nm) was slightly reduced than that of PS template [19]. Besides, the elemental mapping of calcined TiO2 powder, TiO2 opal with PS template (PS-TiO2) and IO-Ti were depicted in Fig. S2. The carbon residue content in IO-Ti (21.6 %) was much lower than that in PS-TiO2 (82.8 %), and it was similar to that in calcined TiO2 (19.5 %), which demonstrated that heat treatment effectively removes PS template. The IO-CTi and IO-CFTi in Fig. 1(C and D) demonstrated that loading CuCQDs and IL-CuCQDs-F did not destroy the inverse opal structure [16]. Additionally, the TEM spectra (Fig. 1E) and EDS elemental mapping (Fig. S3) of IO-CFTi were performed, depicting the Cu and F elements’ uniform distribution on the ordered skeleton, proving the successful introduction and uniform distribution of IL-CuCQDs-F on IO-CFTi. Further magnified TEM spectra (Fig. 1F) and corresponding SAED patterns (Fig. S4) revealed lattice spacings of 0.36, 0.35, and 0.23 nm in IO-CFTi, corresponding to the IL-CuCQDs-F, TiO2 (101), and (001) crystal planes, respectively [14,20,21]. Overall, the inverse opal structure remained unaffected by the uniform distribution of IL-CuCQDs-F on the IO-CFTi.

PXRD was used to characterize the crystallographic structure in Fig. 2 A. The peaks located at 25.4°, 37.9°, 48.1°, 54.1° and 55.2° were assigned to (101), (004), (200), (211), and (105) facets of anatase TiO2, respectively. This is consistent with the standard JCPDF of anatase TiO2 (21-1272), which suggests the presence of TiO2 in these three catalysts [22]. After loading CuCQDs and IL-CuCQDs-F, the crystallinity of IO-CTi and IO-CFTi remained basically the same as that of IO-Ti, demonstrating that the loading of CuCQDs and IL-CuCQDs-F had little influence on the crystal structure of TiO2. To explore the surface area of the three catalysts, the N2 adsorption experiments were conducted (Fig. 2B and Table S1). In Fig. 2B, the as-synthesized catalysts showed type IV isotherms, confirming the existence of the micro-mesoporous structure. IO-CFTi in Table S1 maintained a comparable surface area (274.56 m2·g−1) to that of IO-Ti (276.70 m2·g−1), but surpassed that of IO-CTi (260.02 m2·g−1). And the ratio of S mic/S meso for IO-CFTi (0.97) was notably higher than that of IO-Ti (0.82) and IO-CTi (0.75). A higher S mic/S meso ratio indicates an increase in the specific surface area of micropores relative to the mesoporous specific surface area. This suggests that IL-CuCQDs-F leads to the transformation of mesopores into micropores on IO-CFTi due to the uniform loading of IL-CQDs-F. While the CuCQDs may form larger aggregates that block some pores, resulting in a decrease in specific surface area and a lower S mic/S meso ratio. To further investigate the thermal stability, TGA and DTG curves were evaluated under N2 atmosphere, as displayed in Fig. 2C. After heating to 550 °C, the three catalysts lost 7.4 %, 8.5 %, and 8.9 % of their weight, respectively. The weight loss observed between 40–110 °C and 250–400 °C can be attributed to the loss of adsorbed and chemically bound water, as well as the combustion of carbon sources such as CuCQDs and IL-CuCQFs-F [17]. The results of TGA and DTG curves suggested that the loading of both CuCQDs and IL-CuCQDs-F did not affect the thermal stability of IO-CTi and IO-CFTI. Additionally, the FTIR spectrum of both CuCQDs and IL-CuCQDs-F (Fig. S5) displayed the peaks observed at 654–949, 1100, 1327, 1404, 1511, 1621, 2344, 2778–3023 and 3434 cm−1, corresponding to C3N3, CuN, OH, CN, COO − , CN, CO, CH, and NH bonds, respectively [13,23–25]. The emergence of a new peak in IL-CuCQDs-F at 1169 cm−1 suggested the successful modification of IL-CuCQDs-F by the ionic liquid, attributed to the imidazole ring. The FTIR spectra in Fig. 2D illustrated that IO-Ti, IO-CTi and IO-CFTi have peaks at 1636 and 3399 cm−1, assigned to TiO and OH stretching vibrations of surface hydroxyl groups, respectively [20,26–29]. This indicated the presence of TiO2 in IO-Ti, IO-CTi, and IO-CFTi. Meanwhile, the peaks observed at 892, 1020–1050, 1401, and 2341 cm−1 in IO-CTi and IO-CFTi were attributed to the triazine ring (C3N3), CuN, CN and CO functional groups, respectively, originating from the loaded CuCQDs and IL-CuCQDs-F. Notably, compared to IO-CTi, a new peak at 1205 cm−1 on IO-CFTi, which was ascribed to the imidazolium ring, indicated the successful lading of IL-CuCQDs-F on IO-CFTi.

The XPS spectra and elemental distribution of CuCQDs and IL-CuCQDs-F can be observed in Fig. S6. The spectrum in Fig. S6A illustrated that the CuCQDs exhibited peaks corresponding to N, C, O, and Cu. In addition, a new peak of F was observed in IL-CuCQDs-F, which originated from the ionic liquid [HOEtMIM][BF4]. The C 1 s spectra (Fig. S6B) revealed peaks at 284.4, 285.3, 286.2, and 287.9 eV of CuCQDs, which can be assigned to sp2 C, sp3 C, CN and CO bonds, respectively [20,28,29]. IL-CuCQDs-F also exhibited these four peaks, with the sp2 C content in IL-CuCQDs-F increased from 25 % to 36 %, while the CO bond content decreased from 25 % to 7 % [13,24,30]. Additionally, the sp2 C and CO bond peaks shifting towards lower binding energies by 0.2 and 0.3 eV. This indicated that the addition of ionic liquid promoted electron transfer and enhanced the condensation reaction between the hydroxyl groups on [HOEtMIM][BF4] and the carboxyl groups on EDTA(Cu), resulting in a reduction in the number of CO bonds [31]. In the N 1s spectra (Fig. S6C), the peaks appeared at 399.0 and 399.9 eV in CuCQDs correspond to the amide N originating from EDTA(Cu) and the pyrrolic N generated during the formation of CuCQDs, respectively. The new peak observed at 401.0 eV in IL-CuCQDs-F compared to CuCQDs can be attributed to the imidazole ring N on ionic liquid [HOEtMIM][BF4] [29]. Moreover, the peak intensity of amide N showed a considerable decrease (from 74 % to 28 %). This phenomenon indicated that the ionic liquid inhibits the self-polymerization of EDTA(Cu) and facilitates the dehydration and condensation reaction, leading to more pyrrolic N on IL-CuCQDs-F [32]. Moreover, the binding energies of both amide N and pyrrolic N in IL-CuCQDs-F were decreased, which means the introduction of electron-rich [HOEtMIM][BF4] raised the electron cloud density close to N atoms. The Cu 2p spectrum (Fig. S6D) revealed that the peaks observed at 932.5 and 952.5 eV for CuCQDs and IL-CuCQDs-F could be assigned to Cu 2p3/2 and Cu 2p1/2, thereby suggesting the existence of Cu coordination on CuCQDs and IL-CuCQDs-F [14]. The F 1s spectrum (Fig. S6E) displayed a peak observed at 685.2 eV in the IL-CuCQDs-F, which can be attributed to the B-F bond derived from the [BF4] on ionic liquid [33].

Furthermore, full-scan XPS spectra and elemental distributions of IO-Ti, IO-CTi, and IO-CFTi were depicted in Fig. 3 A and Table S2, respectively. In the XPS spectra of IO-Ti, IO-CTi, and IO-CFTi, all peaks corresponding to C, O, and Ti were observed. The appearance of N and Cu peaks in IO-CTi indicated the presence of CuCQDs. IO-CFTi exhibited an increase in N and Cu contents, along with the appearance of a new peak assigned to F element. The F 1s, Ti 2p, and O 1s spectra for the catalysts were displayed in Fig. 3B-D. The F 1s spectrum of IO-CFTi (Fig. 3B) revealed two distinct peaks at 684.0 and 685.3 eV, which correspond to the TiF and B-F bonds, respectively. The presence of TiF bond indicated that IL-CuCQDs-F have been successfully loaded on IO-CFTi through the TiF bond. And the binding energy of the B-F bond in IO-CFTi moved 0.1 eV in the direction of higher wavelength compared to B-F in IL-CuCQDs-F, which was attributed to the formation of TiF bond promoting the electrons transfer from IL-CuCQDs-F to TiO2 inverse opal. As presented in Ti 2p XPS spectrum (Fig. 3C), the appearance peaks of IO-Ti, IO-CTi, and IO-CFTi at 457.4, 458.4, 460.2 and 464.1 eV were attributed to Ti3+ 2p3/2, Ti4+ 2p3/2, Ti3+ 2p1/2 and Ti4+ 2p1/2, respectively. For O 1s spectrum of IO-Ti, IO-CTi, and IO-CFTi (Fig. 3D), the emerging peaks at 529.6, 531.0, and 532.1 eV belonged to TiO, TiOH, and CO bond, respectively [27,28]. Compared to IO-Ti and IO-CTI, the peaks of Ti4+ 2p3/2, Ti4+ 2p1/2, and TiO of O 1s in IO-CFTi exhibited a shift towards lower binding energy by 0.2 eV, which may be due to the TiF bond facilitating the electron transfer, resulting in an increasing electron cloud density around O and Ti atoms [34]. The N 1s spectrum of IO-CTi (Fig. S6F) revealed two distinct peaks at 398.8 and 399.9 eV, which correspond to amide N and pyrrolic N originating from CuCQDs. The new peak observed at 400.8 eV in IO-CFTi compared to IO-CTi can be attributed to the imidazole ring N. The binding energies of amide N and pyrrolic N in IO-CFTi have been decreased 0.1 and 0.3 eV compared to IO-CTi, indicating the electrons transfer from IL-CuCQDs-F to TiO2 inverse opal.

According to the foregoing analysis, it can be concluded that the IO-CFTi catalyst, possessing an inverse opal structure, had been synthesized successfully. The electronic-rich IL-CuCQDs-F, which have been modified by ionic liquid was strongly connected with IO-Ti through TiF bond. This resulted in a more uniform and firmer loading of IL-CuCQDs-F on IO-CFTi. The TiF bond may improve the separation efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, thereby promoting the capability for photocatalytic CO2 reduction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigating the photocatalytic reduction of CO2
ce:section.ce:para: As illustrated in Fig. 4 A, the IO-CFTi exhibited excellent catalytic performance within 6 h, with a CO production rate of 78.1 μmol·g−1·h−1, and a high CO selectivity of 98 %. The yield of CO on IO-CFTi was equivalent to 1.1–50.1 times that of the literature (Table S3). To confirm that the detected products were indeed from CO2 reduction rather than carbonaceous residue decomposition, control experiments of IO-CFTi were carried out under different conditions [35]. Fig. 4B showed that the excellent CO generation rate was observed only when light, CO2, and IO-CFTi were present together in the system, and any other conditions resulted in a near-zero CO generation rate. Moreover, carbon isotope analysis (13CO) was performed to illustrate the source of CO in Fig. 4C [36]. The reaction products were primarily CO and CH4, with other by-products present at negligible levels of less than 2 %. The detection of both 13CO and CO indicated that the majority of CO originates from photocatalytic carbon dioxide conversion, as the relative intensity of 13CO was approximately 32 times higher than that of CO. The above results revealed that carbon impurities have a negligible impact on the photoreduction of CO2. Fig. 4D depicted the photocatalytic CO2 reduction activity of TiO2, CuCQDs, IL-CuCQDs-F, IO-Ti, IO-CTi, and IO-CFTi. Generally, CuCQDs and IL-CuCQDs-F have no apparent CO2 photoreduction capacity, and pure TiO2 powder has limited catalytic activity and produces mainly CO at a rate of 2.1 μmol·g−1·h−1, consistent with TiO2′s reported performance [37]. IO-Ti had a greater CO generation rate (4.8 μmol·g−1·h−1) compared to pure TiO2, which can be attributed to the inverse opal structure of the IO-Ti [38]. Inverse opal structure can increase the light absorption and photoreaction efficiency that facilitating light collection and utilization. Notably, after loading CuCQDs and IL-CuCQDs-F on IO-Ti, respectively, the CO generation rates of IO-CTi and IO-CFTi significantly increased to 43.5 and 78.1 μmol·g−1·h−1. The generation rate of IO-CFTi was 1.8 times that of IO-CTi, demonstrating that the IL-CuCQDs-F modified by [HOEtMIM][BF4] further enhanced the CO2 photoreduction capacity of IO-CFTi.

The process parameters in the system were studied, and the findings were displayed in Fig. 5 A-C. The CO generation rate of IO-CFTi (Fig. 5A) was affected by the concentration of ionic liquid modified on IL-CuCQDs-F, with the optimal ratio of [HOEtMIM][BF4] being 10 wt%. The excessive ionic liquid may affect the production of the carbon core surface functional groups in IL-CuCQDs-F, resulting in a low CO generation rate. As demonstrated in Fig. 5B, the CO generation rate presented a volcano curve as the doping amount of IL-CuCQDs-F increased from 0.3 wt% to 3.0 wt%, and IO-CFTi displayed the highest CO generation rate when the doping amount of IL-CuCQDs-F was 1.0 wt%. This indicates that the introduction of IL-CuCQDs-F may lead to aggregation, inhibiting the photocatalytic activity of IO-CFTi. In addition, IO-CFTi with different pore sizes can be obtained by adjusting the PS template size, thus influencing the catalytic efficiency of CO2 photoreduction [16]. Fig. 5C displayed that with a PS template size of 250 nm, IO-CFTi reached its maximum CO generation rate, which was 1.78, 1.44, 1.52, and 1.97 times higher than that of IO-CFTi (150), IO-CFTi (200), IO-CFTi (350) and CFTi powder, respectively. This suggested that the various pore sizes also had an impact on photocatalytic activity [38]. In order to investigate the cyclic stability of IO-CFTi, the catalytic experiments were conducted 5 times under the same reaction condition, each lasting 6 h under irradiation (Fig. 5D). The CO generation rate of IO-CFTi declined by only 7.0 % after the fifth cycle. And the SEM image of IO-CFTi after the photocatalytic reaction (Fig. S7) showed that the IO-CFTi maintained inverse opal structure after the photocatalytic reaction, which exhibiting excellent stability and potential for photocatalytic application.

The UV–vis DRS spectra of CuCQDs, IL-CuCQDs-F, IO-Ti, IO-CTi, and IO-CFTi were obtained in Fig. 6 A. The light absorption of CuCQDs was mainly concentrated in the ultraviolet range (300–400 nm), whereas IL-CuCQDs-F showed a distinct absorption peak in the visible range of 400–600 nm due to the imidazole groups in [HOEtMIM][BF4] that promoting the absorption in visible light range [14]. IO-Ti only absorbed UV light, consistent with the previous report that TiO2 can only be excited by UV light [37]. IO-CTi exhibited slightly increased absorption, while IO-CFTi displayed further enhanced visible light absorption. Then the band gaps of CuCQDs, IL-CuCQDs-F, IO-Ti, IO-CTi and IO-CFTi (Fig. S8A-B) were calculated by the Tauc formula (K(hv-Eg)1
/
n = F(R)hv) which were measured at 2.59, 2.24, 2.95, 2.71 and 2.58 eV, respectively [39]. Moreover, the valence band positions (VB) of CuCQDs, IL-CuCQDs-F, IO-Ti, IO-CTi and IO-CFTi were evaluated by VB XPS spectra in Fig. S8C-D, revealing corresponding valence band positions of 0.89, 0.64, 2.35, 2.34, and 2.16 eV, respectively [34]. The valence band position of IO-CFTi (Fig. S8D) had obviously shifted upwards compared to IO-Ti and IO-CTi, which is consistent with the observed changes in valence band position for IL-CuCQDs-F compared to CuCQDs (Fig. S8C). To further elucidate the effect of IL-CuCQDs-F on electronic properties, the CB were calculated by the formula (E CB = E VB − E g). The calculated CB positions for CuCQDs, IL-CuCQDs-F, IO-Ti, IO-CTi, and IO-CFTi were calculated to be 1.70, 1.60, 0.60, 0.37, and 0.42 eV, respectively. These results indicate that the introduction of IL-CuCQDs-F modified by [HOEtMIM][BF4] facilitates effective electron migration from the VB to the CB [40].

To reveal the effect of IO-CFTi with different PS template sizes on photocatalysis, the reflectance spectra of individual photonic domains of IO-CFTi were conducted using optical spectrometer and optical microscope. The reflectance spectra of IO-CFTi (150), IO-CFTi (200), IO-CFTi (250) and IO-CFTi (350) were analysed in Fig. 6B. All IO-CFTi with varying PS template sizes manifested distinct photonic band gaps (PBG) due to the inverse opal structure, continuously shifted towards higher wavelengths as the PS template size increases [38,41]. The electronic band gap (EBG) of IL-CuCQDs-F was 2.24 eV according to Fig. S8, and the corresponding electronic excitation wavelength of IL-CuCQDs-F was calculated to be at ∼550 nm, using the formula for photon energy (E=hc/λ) [16]. Based on the slow photon effect brough about by the inverse opal structure, while the blue-edge of photons on the inverse opal catalyst aligned with the electronic excitation wavelength of the cocatalyst, the light utilization can be significantly improved by increasing the effective optical path length [16,42]. Subsequently, the electronic excitation wavelength region of IL-CuCQDs-F in Fig. 6B only overlapped with the blue-edge of IO-CFTi (250) among various sizes IO-CFTi, resulting in an enhanced slow photon effect, which was consistent with the findings that the IO-CFTi (250) achieved the highest CO2 photoreduction activity in Fig. 5C [19,43,44].

To explore the influence of the electrical conductivity of CuCQDs and IL-CuCQDs-F on photocatalysis, the Raman spectroscopy (Fig. S9) and EIS spectrum (Fig. 6C) of CuCQDs and IL-CuCQDs-F were analysed. The ID /IG ratio of CuCQDs and IL-CuCQDs-F were determined to be 0.69 and 0.65, respectively, in Fig. S9. The smaller ID /IG ratio of IL-CuCQDs-F represented a higher degree of graphitization on IL-CuCQDs-F, suggesting IL-CuCQDs-F had higher electrical conductivity [45]. EIS is used to evaluate the charge separation efficiency of photocatalysts. A smaller EIS arc radius indicates better charge separation and higher electrical conductivity of the catalyst. In Fig. 6C, the EIS results show that the Rct values for CuCQDs and IL-CuCQDs-F were 43 and 32 Ω, respectively, consistent with the Raman spectra and indicating high electrical conductivity. IO-CFTi exhibited the lowest Rct value of 121 Ω (Fig. 6C) compared to IO-Ti and IO-CTi, providing evidence that highly conductive IL-CuCQDs-F enhanced the conductivity of IO-CFTi through the TiF bond [13,28,46]. Furthermore, IO-CFTi exhibited reduced ΔEp and increased ΔIp (Fig. 6D) in comparison to IO-CTi and IO-Ti, demonstrating the noticeably enhanced electron transfer and redox ability. The steady-state photoluminescence (PL) spectra of IO-Ti, IO-CTi and IO-CFTi (Fig. 6E), revealed the weakest PL intensity for IO-CFTi, indicating the accelerated separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs by 10.26 and 8.84 times than that of IO-Ti and IO-CTi, respectively [47,48]. Meanwhile, to investigate the capability of photogenerated electron separation efficiency, the transient photocurrent spectra of IO-Ti, IO-CTi and IO-CFTi were analysed in Fig. 6F. The photocurrent values of IO-CFTi gradually decreased and stabilized, being 3.2 times and 1.7 times greater compared to those of IO-Ti and IO-CTi, respectively, demonstrating that the presence of TiF bond could transfer photogenerated carriers rapidly.

The effective CO2 adsorption facilitates efficient photocatalytic reduction of CO2. Based on an analysis of the isothermal adsorption curves for CO2 (Fig. 7 A), IO-Ti, IO-CTi, IO-CFTi and CFTi powder exhibited CO2 adsorption capacities of 2.84, 6.30, 18.79 and 8.38 cm3·g−1, respectively. The CO2 adsorption capacity of IO-CTi was 2.22 times that of IO-Ti, primarily attributed to the CO2 affinity of the exposed CuN bond, which was formed during the pyrolysis of EDTA(Cu) in the process of CuCQDs manufacture [49,50]. Furthermore, the CO2 adsorption capacity of IO-CFTi was 6.62 and 2.98 times that of IO-Ti and IO-CTi, which indicated that in addition to the CuN bond, the imidazole group on IL-CuCQDs-F derived from the [HOEtMIM][BF4] also significantly enhanced the CO2 adsorption [51,52]. Moreover, the CO2 adsorption capacity on IO-CFTi was 2.24 times that of CFTi powder, suggesting that the 3DOM structure of IO-CFTi facilitated the CO2 adsorption. Based on TGA curves of IO-Ti, IO-CTi and IO-CFTi before and after CO2 adsorption in Fig. S10, the CO2 temperature-programmed desorption (TPD) curves in Fig. 7B were plotted. IO-Ti, IO-CTi, and IO-CFTi showed similar TPD curves before CO2 adsorption. After CO2 saturation, the CO2 desorption peaks occurred at 137.5 °C (IO-Ti), 134.5 °C (IO-CTi), and 133.2 °C (IO-CFTi). IO-CFTi exhibited the highest CO2 desorption amount and the lowest desorption temperature, indicating its superior CO2 adsorption capacity. In brief, the confined grow of IO-CFTi with CuN bond and imidazole group on 3DOM structure of IO-CFTi enhanced the CO2 adsorption, thereby facilitating the process of CO2 photoreduction on IO-CFTi.

To further investigate the activation and reduction process of CO2 molecules, in situ FTIR analysis was performed to detect the reactive intermediates throughout the photocatalytic process in Fig. 8 . The FTIR spectra were collected at different light irradiation time, and several bands appeared as the irradiation time increased from 0 to 30 min. The FTIR spectrum displayed b-CO3 2
− (1288 and 1578 cm−1), m-CO3 2
− (1301, 1491, 1509 and 1542 cm−1), HCO3
− (1458 and 1472 cm−1), and COOH* species (1341, 1523, 1559 and 1717 cm−1) formed during the CO2 photoreduction process [17,53,54]. The adsorbed CO2 was activated as CO2*, which interacted with H2O on the catalyst surface to produce HCO3
− , b-CO3 2
− and m-CO3 2
− intermediates, and then further hydrogenated to form COOH* [8,55]. In addition, the increased intensity of b-CO3 2
− , m-CO3 2
− and COOH* with prolonged irradiation time suggested the absorption and conversion of CO2 molecules into carbon active intermediate species. Among these species, the COOH* served as crucial intermediates and the rate-limiting stage in the CO2 photoreduction process [56]. The adsorbed COOH* was further reduced by reaction with another two electrons and protons, resulting in the generation of CO and H2O. The following presents a probable mechanism for the photocatalytic CO2 reduction by IO-CFTi:
(1) TiO2 → h+(VB) + e − (CB)
(2) IL-CuCQDs-F→IL-CuCQDs-F+ + e −
(3) 4h+ + 2H2O → O2 + 4H+
(4) CO2(g) → CO2*
(5) CO2* + e − + H+ → COOH*
(6) COOH* + e − + H+ → CO* + H2O
(7) CO* → CO

Based on the above analysis, the CO2 photoreduction mechanism of IO-CFTi was revealed in Fig. 9 . Upon light irradiation, IO-CFTi underwent an excited electronic transition that resulted in the formation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs (Eq. (1). The conduction band position of IL-CuCQDs-F (E CB = −1.60 eV) was more negative than that of TiO2 (E CB = −0.60 eV), indicating that the photogenerated electrons of IL-CuCQDs-F can be transferred to the conduction band of IO-CFTi, leading to efficient separation of photogenerated electrons (Eq. (2)). During the photoreduction of CO2, the protons were generated from water (Eq. (3)) and the CO2 molecules were adsorbed and activated as CO2* by IL-CuCQDs-F (Eq. (4)). Then the adsorbed CO2* species underwent reduction by accepting photogenerated electrons and protons, resulting in the formation of the intermediate COOH* (Eq. (5)). The COOH* intermediate was further reduced by accepting electrons and protons, releasing CO* and H2O (Eq. (6)), and CO* desorbed and was converted to CO (Eq. (7)).

In summary, the IL-CuCQDs-F was uniformly self-assembled onto TiO2 inverse opal through a TiF bond to synthesize IO-CFTi successfully. The fluorine-rich IL-CuCQDs-F, which exhibited high visible light absorption, was synthesized using imidazolium ionic liquid [HOEtMIM][BF4] and EDTA(Cu), self-assembled onto TiO2 inverse opal via TiF bond. The robust TiF interface established between the IL-CuCQDs-F and TiO2 inverse opal accelerated the separation and rapid migration of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. Equally important, the enhanced visible light absorption was synergised with the slow photon effect by adjusting the pore size of the inverse opal structure, significantly increasing the absorption and utilization of visible light. Besides, the presence of the CuN bond and imidazole group on the 3DOM structure of IO-CFTi promoted the CO2 adsorption, expediting the CO2 photoreduction process. The synthesized IO-CFTi achieved an improved CO generation rate of 78.1 μmol·g−1·h−1 for CO2 photoreduction equivalent to 1.1–50.1 times that of the literature, demonstrating great potential for efficient CO2 photoreduction applications.
